coredata.dc:title: Machine learning framework for selective and sensitive metal ion sensing with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots heterostructure
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Ruma
Paria, Abhirup
Giri, P.K.
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces a machine learning (ML) framework to optimize photodetector performance for sensor applications. Using the data from the fabricated photodetector with the heterostructure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticles (Au@N-GQDs), various supervised ML models (more than 20 models) are trained and tested for the selection and refinement of the most effective algorithm for our work. Depending on the best-performed ML model, the optimized working wavelength of the photodetector is found for the detection of metal ions. Remarkably, the ML-based sensor shows a high level of selectivity and sensitivity in nM level towards Fe3+ ions in Brahmaputra river water. A strong alignment between model predictions and experimental outcomes validates the efficacy of the proposed ML-based framework. Moreover, data visualization techniques such as heatmaps, classification algorithms, and confusion matrices are introduced to identify the trends in the database. The mechanistic insight of the sensor performance towards Fe3+ ion sensing is further explained with heatmap analysis and experimental verification, which emphasizes the role of photo-induced charge transfer and Fe–O bond formation between metal ions and Au@N-GQDs due to the high electron affinity of Fe3+ ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supervised machine learning
Heatmap matrix
Classification algorithm
Confusion matrix
Metal ion sensing in river water

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of ML-integrated metal ions sensing
ML-integrated Fe3+ ion sensing with mechanistic insight
Detection of metal ions in real-world samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work reported a novel ML-based framework dedicated to evaluating a photodetector performance for metal ion sensing application. We trained various ML models with the dataset generated from plasmonic photodetectors constructed using in-house synthesized Au@N-GQDs heterostructures, and further, we employed advanced data visualization techniques to uncover the underlying patterns. A comprehensive comparative analysis was employed to optimize device parameters, like responsivity, EQE, and sensitivity of the sensor by incorporating various supervised models, including Neural Network, Support Vector Machine, Gaussian Process Regression, Decision Tree, and Linear Regression. The selectivity of the proposed sensor was tested with various classification algorithms, among which the Linear Discriminant algorithm showed high accuracy in prediction performance with a high selectivity towards Fe3+ metal ion. Further, the ML-based prediction revealed a non-linear relation between the photocurrent and the Fe\u00B3\u207A concentration with a wide concentration range (1\u20131000 nM) along with a sensitivity of 1 nM. The mechanistic studies indicate that the high affinity of the sensor for Fe\u00B3\u207A ions is due to the formation of Fe\u2013O bonds and its strong electron affinity. The ML-based study supported the charge transfer mechanism with a heatmap matrix by correlating ion concentration, dark current, light current, and ratio I/I0. A successful implementation of the sensor for detecting metal ions in Brahmaputra River water was also demonstrated, indicating its applicability to real-world applications. We believe that this proposed approach in nanoscience is a very innovative approach for future sensor development economically and rationally.We acknowledge Central Instruments Facility, I.I.T. Guwahati, for providing the TEM, FESEM, and Raman facilities. We thank Dr. H. Sugimoto, Kobe University, Japan, for the support in the XPS measurement.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
A general block diagram of a two-tier machine learning approach for predicting the performance of a photodetector in terms of its spectral responsivity and
E Q E
.
Flow chart of the ML approach for the selection of metal ions and further detection of the metal ion in nM level.

(a) Heatmap matrix for correlating the features for responsivity. (b) Variation of the spectral responsivity with wavelength for experimental and predicted data points. (c) The graph between actual and predicted responsivity follows a straight line, indicating a high accuracy of prediction. (d) Heatmap shows the correlation between wavelength, predicted responsivity (Ŕ), and
E Q E
. (e) Variation of
E Q E
with wavelength for experimental and predicted data points. (f) The actual and predicted
E Q E
graph indicates good agreement between the expected result and the experimental data.

(a) Selectivity of Au@N-GQDs towards various metal ions is assessed with photocurrent (I/I0) ratio in the presence of 1 μM metal ion concentrations. Confusion matrix for the selectivity of different metal ions (Fe3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+), (b) based on the number of observations and (c) TPR and FNR percentage values for each class. (d) The ROC curve for Fe3+ shows a perfect prediction.

(a) Temporal photocurrent response of Au@N-GQDs with varying Fe3+ concentrations (1–1000 nM) under 405 nm pulsed laser excitation. (b) Heat matrix for correlation of features intended for Fe3+ sensing. (c) The variation of photocurrent ratio (I/I0) as a function of Fe3+ concentration: experimental and ML predicted data, and (d) shows the corresponding validation curve.

(a) TEM image of Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+ showing a chain-like structure. (b) A comparison of the Raman spectra of Au@N-GQDs before and after the addition of Fe3+. A comparison of the XPS spectra of (c) O1s, (d) N1s, and (e) Au 4f for different samples. (f) XPS spectrum of Fe 4p for Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+.
Comparison of ML-based sensors for Fe3+ sensing reported in the literature and the present work.

head.ce:title: Machine learning framework for selective and sensitive metal ion sensing with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots heterostructure

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science (Physics), Haldia Institute of Technology, Haldia, 721657, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Computer Science & Engineering (Artificial Intelligence & Machine Learning), Haldia Institute of Technology, Haldia, 721657, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati, 781039, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Nanotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Guwahati, Guwahati -781039, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces a machine learning (ML) framework to optimize photodetector performance for sensor applications. Using the data from the fabricated photodetector with the heterostructure of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticles (Au@N-GQDs), various supervised ML models (more than 20 models) are trained and tested for the selection and refinement of the most effective algorithm for our work. Depending on the best-performed ML model, the optimized working wavelength of the photodetector is found for the detection of metal ions. Remarkably, the ML-based sensor shows a high level of selectivity and sensitivity in nM level towards Fe3+ ions in Brahmaputra river water. A strong alignment between model predictions and experimental outcomes validates the efficacy of the proposed ML-based framework. Moreover, data visualization techniques such as heatmaps, classification algorithms, and confusion matrices are introduced to identify the trends in the database. The mechanistic insight of the sensor performance towards Fe3+ ion sensing is further explained with heatmap analysis and experimental verification, which emphasizes the role of photo-induced charge transfer and Fe–O bond formation between metal ions and Au@N-GQDs due to the high electron affinity of Fe3+ ions.

Machine learning-assisted photodetector for selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in solid phase.
Image 1

ce:text.ce:italic: Metal ion sensing in river water

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, machine learning (ML) has been drawing attention as the most innovative tool to predict complicated events in various scientific fields [1–4]. As a powerful branch of artificial intelligence, ML excels at uncovering hidden patterns within data. ML has received significant consideration due to its extraordinary skill in predicting data trends. The ML-driven model is extensively explored in material science, healthcare, environmental monitoring, and more [1–3]. By employing computational and data mining techniques, ML extracts valuable insights and builds predictive models with outstanding accuracy. These ML models can process massive datasets efficiently, learning from successful and unsuccessful attempts without human error [1,5]. By automating tasks and accelerating processes, ML offers significant time and cost savings compared to traditional methods. The versatility of ML extends in enormous applications, including chemical sensors [6,7] and semiconductor photodetectors [4,8]. Deng et al. reported an ML-assisted dual-mode photodetector with carbon and zinc oxide composite that can respond to near-infrared (NIR) and ultraviolet (UV) light [9]. ML models were reported to design an NIR organic semiconductors-based photodetector depending on previous scientific and theoretical data [4]. Recently, Pandey et al. trained ML models to predict spectral response and detectivity of metal halide perovskite photodetector [8]. For the ML prediction, they prepared a dataset from 150 previous literatures. The k-nearest neighbor (KNN) ML model was used for the experimental data to predict the photodetection behavior of the SbSnSe2 nanosheet [10]. Nowadays, ML is rapidly growing in the field of optoelectronics. While graphitic and plasmonic photodetectors are pivotal in optoelectronics, the application of ML with these materials is comparatively underdeveloped. Moreover, most ML-based studies depend on data from previously published research gathered under diverse conditions. This reliance on heterogeneous datasets could compromise the accuracy of predictive models.

ML is rapidly developing as a key area of exploration in the advancement of the sensor. ML has been effectively combined with nanomaterials to develop predictive models for detecting tetracyclines [11] and various metal ions in aqueous solutions [12–14]. Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been explored as efficient sensors owing to their exceptional morphological and optical behaviors [15–18]. The occurrence of various functional groups, a high aspect ratio, and the biocompatibility of GQDs orientate themselves towards metal ions sensing. Llaver et al. very recently trained an ML algorithm for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions with fluorescent GQDs along with an nM level of sensitivity [19]. For instance, ML-enhanced dual-channel carbon dot (CD) sensor arrays accurately classified four different tetracycline analytes [11]. Additionally, ML algorithms like gradient boosted trees and XGBoost enabled CD-based sensors achieved ∼100 % predictive accuracy in protein and mycotoxin detection, respectively [12,14]. Moreover, ML has been incorporated for the selective detection of various heavy metal ions using carbon nanoparticle arrays [20]. Till now, most ML-based sensing has been mainly focused on liquid-phase sensing. While GQDs-based fluorescence (FL) probes exhibit impressive sensitivity as sensors, their practical application is hindered by several challenges in liquid medium. Solvent effects, complex sample preparation, single-use nature, expenses, wastefulness, and the chances of impurity contamination during liquid-phase experiments pose significant challenges to the repeatability and reproducibility of these reported sensors [21–23].

The excess of non-degradable metals, including heavy metals and biomagnify metals, are severe global issues that have significant consequences for human health and the environment. Various human activities like mining, industrial waste disposal, and agricultural practices using metal-containing pesticides and fertilizers release uncontrolled amounts of metal ions into the environment, disrupting ecosystems and impacting aquatic life [24–26]. These non-degradable metal ions concentrate in organisms throughout the food chain, magnifying their toxicity and significantly threatening human health. Common heavy metals include lead (Pb), zinc (Zn), mercury (Hg), nickel (Ni), cadmium (Cd), copper (Cu), chromium (Cr), arsenic (As), etc. [27,28]. Despite being present in small amounts, these metals pose significant health risks. Alongside the heavy metals, silver (Ag), iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), molybdenum (Mo), boron (B), calcium (Ca), antimony (Sb), cobalt (Co), aluminum (Al), etc., known as biomagnify metals, are frequently found in the environment. These metal ions are indestructible and tend to accumulate in living organs through the food chain [29]. The increasing concentration of these substances in the tissues of organisms, known as biomagnification, leads to abnormal activity in living objects. Though the metal ions such as Fe, Ca, B, Mn, etc. are essential ions for the healthy growth of the biological system [21,30], the unusual concentration of such ions in the human body was reported to cause anaemia, hemochromatosis, Parkinson's illness, Alzheimer's disease, cancer, and so on [21,31]. Metal ions are necessary for plant growth and various microbial processes in soil ecosystems [32]. They are crucial in nutrient cycling and maintaining healthy soil conditions. Thus, efficient detection of heavy metal ions and biomagnification of metal ions are crucial for healthy living. Not only in the human body but also in the environment, such as drinking water, river water, pond water, wastewater, etc., determining the concentration of such metal ions is essential.

The present study proposed a ML-based framework to study photodetector performance for sensor applications. To train ML models, the datasets are collected from a plasmonics photodetector fabricated with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot and gold nanoparticles (Au@N-GQDs) heterostructure in our lab. Data visualization analysis such as heatmap matrix, classification algorithm, and confusion matrix are used to identify the trends in the database. Depending on various parameters, a comparative analysis of more than 20 supervised ML models is conducted to select the most predictive algorithm, which is subsequently fine-tuned for optimal performance. Finally, the reliability of the proposed framework is validated by successfully detecting Fe3+ metal ions with 100 % selective prediction in Brahmaputra river water with nM level of sensitivity. A strong correlation between experimental results and model predictions demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed approach. Finally, the reason behind the high selectivity and sensitivity of Fe3+ ion sensing with Au@N-GQDs is elucidated due to the new bond formation and charge transfer between Fe3+ ions and Au@N-GQDs.

A novel two-tiered ML approach is proposed here to evaluate the photodetector performance. The performance of a photodetector is defined based on the spectral responsivity (
R s
) and external quantum efficiency (
E Q E
). Thus, the prediction of the photodetector performance is carried out by the prediction of
R s
and
E Q E
using a flowchart presented in Fig. 1 . This methodology leverages the complementary strengths of data-driven learning and physical understanding to achieve accurate and efficient prediction of
R s
and
E Q E
. The datasets are prepared with the measurement of the photocurrent (I) of the photodetector with different wavelengths (λ), input power (Pin) of the incident light, and the associated
R s
and
E Q E
. In Tier I, the ML model is trained using
R s
as output and wavelength of the incident light (λ), input power, and photocurrent as input. The trained ML model can then predict responsivity (R′) for new inputs (λ, input power, and photocurrent), effectively learning the underlying relationship between λ, incident power, and
R s
. The predicted responsivity R′ obtained from Tier I, along with λ, are further used as inputs for the prediction of
E Q E
. In Tier II, the predicted responsivity (R′) and the corresponding λ are used as input for the prediction of
E Q E
. The predicted
E Q E
and the experimental
E Q E
are compared to

test the accuracy of the models. Further, the optimized conditions of photodetector are implemented for the metal ion sensing using the flowchart shown in Fig. 2 . For the prediction of selectivity of the metal ions, the measured light current and dark current and the intensity ratio of the photocurrent (I0/I, I0 and I are the photocurrents before and after the addition of the metal ions, respectively) with metal ions are used as input, and the name of the metal ions are taken as output. Using the classification algorithm, the metal ions with the best-predicted selectivity is further sent for the prediction of sensitivity. For the prediction of the sensitivity, light current, dark current, and the concentration of selected metal ions are taken as input, and the ratio of the photocurrent (I/I0) is taken as output. Finally, the predicted I/I0 and experimental (I/I0) are compared to understand the accuracy of the models.

In the proposed ML framework, all the data were collected from our experimental result. At first, the materials were synthesized in our laboratory for the fabrication of the photodetector. The details of the materials synthesis and their characterization and fabrication are discussed in supporting information, Section S1-S4(SI†). Based on the optical absorption properties of the materials as discussed in Section S5 (SI†), Au@N-GQDs were implemented as an efficient material for photodetection application. Subsequently, for the prediction of the photodetector performance, the required data was collected by I–V measurements, discussed in Section S6 (SI†). A microprobe station (ECOPIA, EPS-500), along with a source-measure unit (Keithley 2400) controlled by LabTracer software, facilitated the electrical characterization of the photodetector. The photodetector was mounted on an optical table in a dark chamber to minimize vibrational noise. For the photocurrent measurements, a 405 nm laser beam and a 250 W xenon lamp (Newport, USA) attached to a monochromator (Oriel Instruments, USA) were used. A chopper (SRS, USA) was also used to generate light pulses. To acquire the experimental data for responsivity (
R s
) and external quantum efficiency (
E Q E
), we used the following steps. From our previous work [22], we know that photocurrent,
I
, is
(1)
I ∝
P
i n
(2)
So , I =
R s
P
i n
Where
P
i n
is the incident power,
R s
is the proportionality constant, known as spectral responsivity.
(3)
R s
=
I
P
i n
(4)
E Q E =
n o .
o f
c o l l e c t e d
e l e c t r o n s
n o .
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
=
R e
R P
Where,
(5)
R P
=
P
i n
E
=
P
i n
λ
h c

and
(6)
R e
=
I q
(7)
E =
h c
λ
Here,
E
= energy of a photon,
q
= electronic charge =
1.6 ×
10
− 19
Coulomb,
h
= Planck's constant,
c
= velocity of light in free space.
(8)
E Q E =
I q
P
i n
λ
h c
(9)
=
h c
q λ
(
I
P
i n
)
=
h c
q λ
R s
(10)
= 1.24 ×
10
− 7
R s
λ
(11)
=
1240
R s
λ
(
n m
)

The values of
R s
and
E Q E
were calculated using equations (3) and (11), respectively, with the experimentally obtained
λ
,
I
and
P
i n
, and further, they were used for ML prediction. Noteworthy, the consistency and reproducibility of the device were tested experimentally before using the photodetector as a sensor. We leveraged a ML classifier to understand better the sensor's selectivity towards a specific metal ion. Various metal ions, such as Fe3+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+, were tested with the proposed sensor. The selectivity performance of the photodetector-based sensor was examined individually with a 1.0 μM concentration of each metal ion by measuring the photocurrent. For the determination of sensor sensitivity, the photocurrent response was further measured with the addition of various concentrations of the selected metal ion under 405 nm laser excitation with power 3.68 mW/cm2 and 5 V bias voltage using a microprobe station at room temperature.

In the present work, various ML models were used to predict the trajectory of the process, and the ML models were implemented using MATLAB. To find out the correlation of various parameters, the heatmap matrix was used. The dataset of the correlated parameters was further used for the prediction of the required parameters. The datasets were randomly partitioned for model development into training (80 %) and validation (20 %) subsets. Various ML models like Neural Network (NN), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Gaussian Process Regression (GPR), Decision Tree (DT), Linear Regression (LR), etc., were used here for the prediction of the sensor performance. The ML classifier with confusion matrix and receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve were introduced here for the prediction of the selectivity of the metal ion sensor. Further, a 5-fold cross-validation method was used to test the stability of the metal ion sensor in the ML approach.

The efficiency of predictions is a vital aspect of ML analysis. For measuring the efficacy of an ML model, Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), and R-squared (R2) are used as evaluation metrics. The values of MAE, MSE, and RMSE tending to zero, and the R2 value = 1 ensures that a model works correctly and reliably. The metrics are described with the following equations:
(12)
M A E =
1 k
∑
i = 1
k
|
x i
r e a l
−
x i
p r e d i c t
|
(13)
M S E =
1 k
∑
i = 1
k
(
x i
r e a l
−
x i
p r e d i c t
)
2
(14)
R M S E =
M S E
=
1 k
∑
i = 1
k
(
x i
r e a l
−
x i
p r e d i c t
)
2
(15)
R 2
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
k
(
x i
r e a l
−
x i
p r e d i c t
)
2
∑
i = 1
k
(
x i
r e a l
−
x
m e a n
)
2
Here,
x i
r e a l
is the data point in the experimental dataset,
x i
p r e d i c t
is the corresponding data point obtained by prediction,
x
m e a n
is the mean value of
x
r e a l
, and
k
is the number of data points.

For the purpose of responsivity prediction, the correlation between various parameters, like wavelength, input power, photocurrent, and responsivity, is examined using heatmap analysis. The heatmap is a very useful tool for visualizing the correlations between the factors influencing the responsivity prediction. The heatmap in Fig. 3 (a) shows the symmetrical nature of the self-correlation axis. This heatmap demonstrates strong positive correlations (∼1) between wavelength and itself and a moderate positive correlation between wavelength and responsivity (0.52). This suggests that as wavelength increases, responsivity tends to grow as well. However, a weak positive correlation exists between responsivity and photocurrent (0.37). Conversely, input power negatively correlates with responsivity (−0.46) and wavelength (−0.18), which indicates that a high responsivity value in a higher wavelength region can be achieved with low input power only. From the heatmap, it is perceived that photocurrent is affected mainly by input power, while the wavelength has a tiny impact on photocurrent. Twenty-three ML models are used here to predict responsivity, and the corresponding results are shown in Table S1 (SI†), along with RMSE, R2, MSE, and MAE values. The Matern 5/2 GPR model achieves the most accurate prediction among twenty-three models. This model yields an RMSE of 0.026, indicating a slight difference between predicted (R′) and actual responsivity (
R s
) values. Additionally, the R2 value of 0.99 suggests that this Matern 5/2 GPR model successfully explains ∼99 % of the variance in the dataset with ∼1 % deviation between experimental and predicated data. Additionally, MSE of 0.0006 and MAE of 0.017 solidify the strong performance of this model in predicting the responsivity with minimal errors. Fig. 3(b) shows a comparative spectral response of experimental and predicted data for the Matern 5/2 GPR model. In the Matern 5/2 GPR model, the validation graph shows a straight line with slope 1, as observed in Fig. 3(c), confirming a high level of accuracy in the prediction [2]. The heatmap of the wavelength, predicted responsivity (R′), and
E Q E
features in Fig. 3(d) revealed a strong correlation between wavelength and predicted responsivity (R′), similar to Tire I. However, the relationship between wavelength and
E Q E
is negative, with a value of −0.46. This suggests that as the wavelength increases, the
E Q E
value decreases. The predicted responsivity perfectly correlates with itself, indicating the correct heatmap calculation. Based on heatmap analysis, several ML algorithms (twenty-four) were tested for the prediction of
E Q E
. The result is shown in Table S2 (SI†). Though most of the models show good prediction, the TNN function obtains a minimum RMSE value of 5.0008 along with R2 of 0.99, MSE of 25.008, and MAE of 3.597, indicating an excellent agreement of the predicted and actual
E Q E
values. Noteworthy, only ∼1 % deviation is observed between experimental and predicated
E Q E
values. The experimental
E Q E
and the TNN-predicted
E Q E
data points are shown in Fig. 3(e) with different wavelengths, confirming the high prediction quality. Moreover, the best-performed TNN ML model shows that Au@N-GQDs based photodetector provides maximum
E Q E
value at ∼590 nm excitation, agreeing nicely with experimental data. For more visualization of the agreement of the experimentally obtained data and TNN algorithm predicted data of
E Q E
(%), the actual vs. predicted response is shown in Fig. 3(f) with a slope of one. This Au@N-GQDs-based photodetector performance is predicted with high accuracy (>98 %) by the proposed framework. With the best predicted EQE value at different wavelengths, the TNN model decides the wavelength of the incident light where EQE is maximum, indicating the best performance condition of the device. Depending on the ML prediction, further metal ion sensing is carried out with the optimized wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of ML-integrated metal ions sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high consistency and reproducibility of photodetector performance (discussedin Section S6, SI†), an Au@N-GQDs photodetector is further executed for the ML-based sensing of various metal ions in the solid state. According to the ML prediction, the optimum working wavelength of Au@N-GQDs-based photodetector is obtained at ∼590 nm. For the nM level of metal ion sensing application, a laser excitation of 590 nm would be better than the Xenon lamp excitation due to its low photocurrent signal. However, due to the unavailability of a 590 nm laser source, we used a 405 nm laser source for sensing metal ions throughout. We believe that the sensing of Fe3+ ions will be more efficient with 590 nm laser excitation. The temporal response of the photocurrent (for 405 nm laser excitation, with power 3.68 mW/cm2) of various metal ions with 1 μM concentration is shown in Fig. S6 (SI†), and the relative changes of the photocurrent, I₀/I (I₀ and I are the photocurrents before and after the addition of the metal ions, respectively) of various metal ions are presented in Fig. 4 (a). Using dark current, light current, and I0/I as input and the name of ions as output, twenty-three various ML classifier algorithms are used to classify different metal ions. The classification accuracy with various models is shown in Table S3 (SI†). Among them, the Linear Discriminant Classifier shows the highest accuracy (∼75.9 %) for the ion selection. A confusion matrix is a helpful table that assists in visualizing the performance of a classification model in ML. It summarizes how well the model performed on a set of test datasets. Here, the confusion matrixes are presented based on the number of observations and percentage values of True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Negative Rate (FNR) for each class of metal ions, as presented in Fig. 4(b and c), respectively. TPR and FNR provide valuable insights into how well the classification model performs, particularly regarding the ability of the model to identify positive class data points and avoid missing them, respectively. In the present case, the Linear Discriminant classifier correctly identifies all the Fe3+ with a TPR of 100 %, whereas the TPR values of other ions are lower than 90 %. This is in good agreement with our experimental selectivity. The ROC curve is a visualization tool used to assess the efficiency of binary classification models' sensitivity and specificity for various threshold probability values. Fig. 4(d) shows the ROC curve for Fe3+ with the area under the curve (AUC) of 1.0, indicating a perfect classification of Fe3+. For other metal ions (Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, and Hg2+), the obtained AUC values are less than 1.0, demonstrating their low selectivity by Au@N-GQDs. The ROC curves, along with AUC values of other ions, are shown in Fig. S7 (SI†).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ML-integrated Fe3+ ion sensing with mechanistic insight
ce:section.ce:para: As the proposed photodetector-based sensor shows a high response for Fe3+ ions, further, the sensor is optimized for Fe3+ ions only. Fe3+ plays a vital role with broad applications across biology, medicine, environmental science, and industry. Fe3+ ion is a crucial transition metal ion in biological systems, playing indispensable roles in various physiological and pathological processes, including oxygen uptake and transport, cellular metabolism, enzyme catalysis, electron transfer, and DNA/RNA synthesis. However, maintaining optimal levels of Fe³⁺ is crucial for healthy living. Deficiency of Fe3+ ions can lead to anemia, characterized by a reduction of hemoglobin synthesis, resulting in fatigue, diminished immunity, and impaired oxygen delivery to tissues. On the other hand, excess Fe3+ ions can be equally harmful. Excess Fe3+ ions can catalyze the formation of harmful reactive oxygen species through the Fenton reaction, damaging cellular components and contributing to diseases such as cancer, hepatitis, hemochromatosis, and organ dysfunction [33]. Additionally, recent research has linked elevated Fe3+ levels to neurodegenerative disorders like Parkinson's, Huntington's, and Alzheimer's diseases [22]. Fe3+ ions also play a significant role in natural aquatic systems and seawater. For instance, iron-humic substance aggregates can effectively adsorb trace metals, while Fe3+ ions can bind organic matter to clay particles. These interactions influence the overall water environment, affecting the distribution and fate of various contaminants [34]. Given the critical role of Fe3+ ions in biological and environmental systems, there has been a growing demand for selective and sensitive methods to accurately quantify their concentrations.

For the detection of Fe3+ ions, the photocurrent response of Au@N-GQDs is measured with the addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions in a broad concentration window (1–1000 nM) at room temperature. Fig. 5 (a) depicts the temporal photocurrent response of Au@N-GQDs with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions. This sensor consistently produced highly stable signals over time when exposed to different Fe3+ concentrations, indicating exceptional efficiency as a solid-phase tool for detecting Fe3+ ions, with a strong perspective for practical applications. The correlation between several parameters like concentration of Fe3+ ion, dark current, light current, and I/I0 in the sensing of Fe3+ ions are intended with the heatmap analysis (see Fig. 5(b)). Note that heatmap analysis is highly beneficial in getting the optimum condition for sensing by considering each parameter contribution. The heatmap reveals that with a higher concentration of Fe3+ ions, the dark current increases while the light current reduces. Under light exposure, the photo-induced charges in Au@N-GQDs preferentially transfer to nearby Fe3+ ions instead of flowing toward the electrodes. This results in a reduction of the measured photocurrent. However, incorporating Fe3+ ions into Au@N-GQDs might lead to the formation of new electronic states that can act as trap states. These trap states capture/emit electrons and holes and later allow them to recombine, causing a rise in the dark current. Thus, with increasing concentration of Fe3+, dark current also increases. The heatmap specifies that I/I0 has a robust negative correlation with the concentration of Fe3+, which is the critical factor for the present sensing process. Twenty different ML algorithms are applied to predict I/I0 with varying concentrations of Fe3+ ion, dark current, and light current. Table S4 (SI†) summarizes the results of all twenty models. Among these, Stepwise Linear model prediction shows a very high accuracy with R2 value of 0.98, RMSE of 0.02089, MSE of 0.000436, and MAE of 0.01984. The R2 value indicates that there is a minimal (∼2 %) deviation between experimental and predicated I/I0. The relative change in the photocurrent, I/I0, with various concentrations of Fe3+ ions is displayed in Fig. 5(c). The ML prediction reveals a non-linear reduction of the photocurrent as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increases. This trend is remarkably consistent with the experimental data. While our ML model is trained on a limited dataset, we expect it to generalize well with a large number of datasets because the validation results are promising, as displayed in Fig. 5(d). As the ML model can predict future data without performing experiments, the ML can be used for Fe3+ sensing, even beyond the experimental data region. This research introduces a groundbreaking solid-state sensor that surpasses the limitations of conventional fluorescence-based metal ion detectors. By integrating a photodetector and machine learning prediction, our sensor achieves a nM level of sensitivity for Fe3+ ions. A comparative performance between previous works and the present work for Fe3+ sensors is presented in Table 1 . It is evident that this work shows a unique solid phase detection with a very high sensitivity.

The stability of the ML-based metal ion sensor is assessed using a 5-fold cross-validation process. The evaluation metrics MAE, MSE, RMSE, and R2 for I/I0 prediction remain remarkably consistent across different folds (see Fig. S8 (a-d),SI†), demonstrating high stability of the sensor performance in ML prediction. To evaluate the experimental stability, the photocurrent of bare Au@N-GQDs and Au@N-GQDs/1 μM Fe3+ were measured over 45 days. Both exhibited negligible photocurrent change compared to the initial measurements, as presented in Fig. S9 (a, b) (SI†). Noteworthy, the addition of Fe³⁺ ions did not compromise stability, even after a prolonged time gap.

A quantitative analysis of the sensor's response towards Fe3+ ions is crucial to elucidate its non-linearity and high selectivity. This analysis will provide valuable insights into the sensor's working mechanism. Here, the possible mechanisms are presented with experimental verification. TEM imaging in Fig. 6 (a) reveals the attachment of Fe3+ ions to Au@N-GQDs, forming a chain-like structure. As observed in Fig. S10 (SI†), bare Fe3+ ion contents show rod-like structures that are well separated from each other; interestingly, with the addition of Au@N-GQDs, the Fe3+ ions come closer and attach to the surface of Au@N-GQDs, leading to a chain-like structure. For a better understating, EDX analysis and the elemental mapping of Au@N-GQDs and Fe3+ complex are depicted in Figs. S11(a–f) (SI†). EDX analysis result shown in the inset of Fig. S11(a) (SI†) demonstrates the presence of C, O, N, Au along with Fe. From Figs. S11(b–f) (SI†), the subsistence of each element (Au, C, N, O, and Fe) and their appearance are confirmed. The Raman spectra of bare Fe3+ content and Au@N-GQDs without and with Fe3+ are presented in Fig. 6(b). In bare Fe3+, the characteristic Raman peak appears at 250 cm−1 [53]. Au@N-GQDs exhibit characteristic D and G bands in their Raman spectra at ∼1347 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1, respectively. Introducing Fe3+ ions to Au@N-GQDs leads to a significant fall in the intensity of the D band. Additionally, a blue shift is observed in the position of this D band. These observations are attributed to the complex formation between the functional groups of Au@N-GQDs and Fe3+ ions. In contrast, the observed redshift of the G band from 1596 cm−1 to 1584 cm−1 (Fig. 6(b)) suggests a drop in the charge density of graphitic components in Au@N-GQDs in the presence of Fe3+ [22]. Thus, the Raman spectra confirm the charge transportation from Au@N-GQDs to Fe3+ via complex formation with the functional/defect states of Au@N-GQDs. In Fig. S12 (SI†), a drastic reduction of the XRD peak intensity of Au (111) indicates the attachment of N-GQDs to the Au (111) facet actively participating in the interaction with Fe3+. For the detailed structural analysis, a comparison of XPS spectra of O 1s and N 1s of N-GQDs, Au@N-GQDs, and Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 6(c and d). In the deconvoluted O 1s spectra of Fig. 6(c), three different peaks at ∼530.3, 531.5, and 532.8 eV confirm the occurrence of oxygen-enriched groups –CO, ‒C-OH/‒C-O-C‒ and –COOH, respectively, in N-GQDs. For Au@N-GQDs, the binding energy of the functional group is observed to be lower (See Table S5, SI†). After the attachment of Fe3+ ions with various oxygen-rich groups of Au@N-GQDs, the binding energy of –COOH reduces, whereas the binding energy of –CO and ‒C-OH/‒C-O-C‒ increases. A new peak appears at ∼529.6 eV due to the Fe–O bond formation [54]. This new bond endorses the formation of new energy states that may behave as trap states, as claimed previously for the upsurge of the dark current with increasing concentration of Fe3+. It is also observed that the contribution of ‒C-OH/‒C-O-C‒ assembly diminishes in Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+ compared to Au@N-GQDs. Hence, it is clear that ‒C-OH/‒C-O-C‒ functional groups play a vital role in complex formation with Fe3+. In N 1s spectra (see Fig. 6(d)), the binding energy at ∼397.4, 99.2, 401.5, and 402.7 eV are assigned as the binding energy of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively, in N-GQDs [22]. For Au@N-GQDs, the binding energy of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N is observed to reduce (see Table S5, SI†), while there is no change in the binding energy of graphitic N. Hence, pyridinic N and pyrrolic N mostly participate in the Au@N-GQDs formation and acquire a higher electronic charge from electron-rich Au NPs. Note that the increased contribution of graphitic N in Au@N-GQDs endorses the decrease of defects/functional groups (consistent with XRD analysis), responsible for the enhancement of the electrical conductivity [55], which is good for Au@N-GQDs to be used as a photodetector. After the addition of Fe3+ ions to Au@N-GQDs, the binding energy of the graphitic N remains constant, revealing that graphitic N has negligible contribution to the complex formation with Fe3+ ions. Very interestingly, the binding energy of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N are observed to change in Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+, confirming that the pyridinic N and pyrrolic N transfer electronic charge to Fe3+ ions via the strong interaction. Noteworthy, the contribution of pyrrolic N is observed to reduce from ∼78.2 % to 53.5 % after the attachment of Fe3+ with Au@N-GQDs. This reveals that pyrrolic N shows a higher influence than pyridinic N towards Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+complex formation. The Au 4f spectra of Au@N-GQDs and Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+ are presented in Fig. 6(e). For Au@N-GQDs, deconvolution of the Au 4f doublet in the XPS spectrum shows two Gaussian peaks centered at ∼83.3 and 87.0 eV. These peaks correspond to the 4f7/2 and 4f5/2 electronic states of Au, respectively, with a spin-orbit splitting of ∼3.7 eV [56]. The appearance of a peak at ∼84.3 eV alongside the metallic Au identified as Au+1 4f7/2 state, endorsing N-GQDs-Au bond formation, as reported in a previous study [56]. After the addition of Fe3+ with Au@N-GQDs, the binding energy of all the states is observed to upturn, which may be due to the delocalization of charge by efficient charge transport from Au NPs to Fe3+ via N-GQDs, leading to the stabilization of Au@N-GQDs. Fig. 6(f) shows the deconvoluted spectrum of Fe 4p of Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+. The binding energy of ∼710.0, 714.0, and 723.6 eV are assigned as the binding energy of Fe 2p3/2, Fe2+, and Fe 2p1/2, respectively [54]. The existence of Fe2+ confirms that Fe3+ accepted electrons from Au@N-GQDs. Thus, Fe3+ receives electrons from Au@N-GQDs by the complex formation with Au@N-GQDs through ‒C-OH/‒C-O-C‒ functional groups, and pyridinic N and pyrrolic N helps Fe3+ to achieve a stable metallic structure by electron transfer. Here, adding Au NPs with N-GQDs enhances the charge transfer and, subsequently, helps to achieve the nM level of sensitivity of Au@N-GQDs based sensor towards Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions in real-world samples
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the proposed sensor was tested using the local river water of Brahmaputra, Guwahati, India. For the testing with the river water, the sample was prepared by following the process as reported in our previous work [22]. After filtration of the collected river water with 0.22 μM membrane, the river water was diluted twenty times with MQ water, and then the diluted solution was mixed with Au@N-GQDs. Afterward, different metal ions (Fe3+, Al3+, and Hg2+) with 100 nM concentration were mixed with Au@N-GQDs solution under sonication, and the prepared solutions were implemented for the measurement of the photocurrent by following the process as discussed in Section S2 (SI†). Fig. S13(a) (SI†) shows a significant change in the photocurrent only for Fe3+ ions. Moreover, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (10–100 nM) were also tested in the river water, as presented in Fig. S13(b) (SI†), revealing a continuous reduction of the photocurrent with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions. The sensor exhibited a highly stable temporal response to varying Fe³⁺ concentrations in river water samples, demonstrating its potential as an efficient and promising solid-phase sensor for real-world Fe³⁺ detection.

This work reported a novel ML-based framework dedicated to evaluating a photodetector performance for metal ion sensing application. We trained various ML models with the dataset generated from plasmonic photodetectors constructed using in-house synthesized Au@N-GQDs heterostructures, and further, we employed advanced data visualization techniques to uncover the underlying patterns. A comprehensive comparative analysis was employed to optimize device parameters, like responsivity,
E Q E
, and sensitivity of the sensor by incorporating various supervised models, including Neural Network, Support Vector Machine, Gaussian Process Regression, Decision Tree, and Linear Regression. The selectivity of the proposed sensor was tested with various classification algorithms, among which the Linear Discriminant algorithm showed high accuracy in prediction performance with a high selectivity towards Fe3+ metal ion. Further, the ML-based prediction revealed a non-linear relation between the photocurrent and the Fe³⁺ concentration with a wide concentration range (1–1000 nM) along with a sensitivity of 1 nM. The mechanistic studies indicate that the high affinity of the sensor for Fe³⁺ ions is due to the formation of Fe–O bonds and its strong electron affinity. The ML-based study supported the charge transfer mechanism with a heatmap matrix by correlating ion concentration, dark current, light current, and ratio I/I0. A successful implementation of the sensor for detecting metal ions in Brahmaputra River water was also demonstrated, indicating its applicability to real-world applications. We believe that this proposed approach in nanoscience is a very innovative approach for future sensor development economically and rationally.

Ruma Das: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Abhirup Paria: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. P.K. Giri: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge Central Instruments Facility, I.I.T. Guwahati, for providing the TEM, FESEM, and Raman facilities. We thank Dr. H. Sugimoto, Kobe University, Japan, for the support in the XPS measurement.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Material synthesis, device fabrication, characterization, TEM, AFM, XRD and UV–visible absorption of N-GQDs, Au@GQDs, Schematic of photocurrent measurement, (I–V) characteristics of Au@N-GQDs, reproducibility of the Au@N-GQDs sensing, the temporal response of Au@N-GQDs with the presence of different metal ions, ROC curve of various ions, stability testing, TEM image of FeCl3, STEM image of Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+ and the corresponding mapping, comparison of XRD spectra of Au@N-GQDs, Fe3+, and Au@N-GQDs/Fe3+, Tables of the ML analysis data for responsivity,
E Q E
, I/I0 and selectivity, Table of fitting parameters of XPS spectra.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2024.119800.
